planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; fresh seeds planted immediately after collecting in fall also establish reliably. All parts of the plant are toxic — the seeds especially so.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; red buckeye is one of the finest native shrubs for the shaded garden, producing upright panicles of brilliant scarlet-red tubular flowers in April and May precisely timed to the return of ruby-throated hummingbirds from migration. The flowers are visited almost exclusively by hummingbirds and are among the most important early-season hummingbird plants available. It grows naturally as a forest understory shrub throughout the Southeast and performs exceptionally well in zone 8–9 Gulf Coast gardens in partial shade. The glossy, palmate compound leaves are attractive all season but the plant goes fully dormant in summer heat — mark its location and interplant with later-season companions. The large, mahogany-colored seeds are toxic to humans and livestock.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade; this plant grows naturally in very acidic, boggy, waterlogged conditions and will not thrive in alkaline or neutral soils.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp cyrilla (also called titi or leatherwood) is a distinctive multi-season native shrub of coastal plain bogs, swamp margins, and pocosins in the Southeast, producing long racemes of small white flowers in June and July that are intensely fragrant and among the most important summer nectar sources for native bees and the basis of the highly prized 'titi honey' produced by southeastern beekeepers. The semi-evergreen leaves turn brilliant orange-red in fall before dropping, and in warm winters may persist through spring. The plant is adapted to very acidic, poorly drained soils where it has little competition. An outstanding specimen for bog gardens, rain gardens, and acid pond margins in zone 7–9.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, very acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; purchase only from nursery-propagated sources — galax has been over-collected from the wild for the floral trade and is declining in many areas.

directions: Plant in moist, very acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; galax is one of the premier evergreen groundcovers for deep shade in acidic woodland gardens. The round, glossy, leathery leaves form a dense, weed-suppressing carpet that turns brilliant bronze-wine-red through fall and winter before returning to rich green in spring — providing bold, year-round color in the shade garden. The small white flowers on tall wands in late spring are attractive to bees. It grows naturally on the rocky, acidic woodland slopes of the Southern Appalachians, where it is sometimes called 'coltsfoot.' Galax leaves are highly prized in the floral industry and are heavily wild-harvested — purchase only nursery-propagated plants. Spreads slowly but persistently by rhizome to form colonies.

planting.note: Plant divisions immediately upon receiving in early spring in very acidic, moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; this plant is difficult to establish and should be purchased only from reputable nursery-propagated sources as it is protected in many states.

directions: Plant in moist, very acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; painted trillium is widely regarded as the most beautiful of the eastern trilliums, producing white flowers with a vivid magenta-pink V-shaped painted mark at the base of each petal in late spring that gives the plant its common name. It grows in coniferous and mixed forest understories with strongly acidic soil, particularly in association with hemlock and pine. It is more demanding of soil acidity than the common great white trillium (T. grandiflorum) and more difficult to establish in the garden. Purchase only nursery-propagated divisions from reputable sources, as wild populations are declining due to collection pressure and deer browsing. Very slow from seed — divisions from established colonies are the only practical propagation method.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; pagoda dogwood needs some protection from afternoon sun in zones 6–7 and is best grown in northern and mountain climates.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; pagoda dogwood is a striking native understory tree distinguished from flowering dogwood (Cornus florida) by its horizontally tiered, pagoda-like branching structure and alternate (rather than opposite) leaves — hence both common names. The flat-topped clusters of small creamy-white flowers in late spring attract many native bee species, and the blue-black berries on red stems in late summer are highly attractive to migrating warblers and thrushes. The plant thrives in cool, moist, shaded conditions and performs best in zones 3–6; it struggles in southern heat and is not suited to zone 8 and south. Fall foliage is an attractive rose-burgundy. The horizontal branching structure is most dramatic in winter silhouette, making it a year-round specimen tree.
